title: Ways of Seeing
author: John Berger
description: Essays on how we look at art and what it means to see. Berger examines the relationship between seeing and knowing — direct, rigorous, and still relevant.

title: Nervous Conditions
author: Tsitsi Dangarembga
description: Set in colonial Rhodesia, Tambu's determination to gain an education exposes the tensions between tradition and modernity. A cornerstone of Zimbabwean literature.

title: Entangled Life
author: Merlin Sheldrake
description: An examination of fungi — the organisms that sustain ecosystems, decompose matter, and connect the living world in ways we are only beginning to understand.

title: Black Skin, White Masks
author: Frantz Fanon
description: An analysis of the psychology of racism and colonialism. Fanon examines how colonised people internalise the values of the oppressor. Direct, unflinching, enduring.

title: Braiding Sweetgrass
author: Robin Wall Kimmerer
description: Indigenous knowledge and botanical science brought together. Kimmerer writes about reciprocity between humans and plants with precision and care.

title: Ways of Curating
author: Hans Ulrich Obrist
description: Obrist traces connections between exhibitions, conversations, and the practice of bringing ideas into proximity. A personal account of curating over four decades.

title: Rousing the Rubble
author: David Hammons
description: A major survey of David Hammons' work — street interventions, sculptures from found materials, and performances that challenge the art world's conventions with wit and precision.

title: The Friendliest Black Artist in America
author: Pope.L
description: Documentation of Pope.L's crawls, interventions, and endurance performances. Work that sits between absurdity and protest, body and pavement, humor and pain.

title: Kerry James Marshall: Mastry
author: Kerry James Marshall
description: A comprehensive monograph of Marshall's paintings — monumental figurative works that insist on Black presence within the Western painting tradition. Rigorous, beautiful, necessary.

title: Jean-Michel Basquiat
author: Leonhard Emmerling
description: An overview of Basquiat's explosive career — graffiti, neo-expressionism, and the raw energy of a self-taught artist who changed contemporary art. Crown, skull, code.

title: My Complement, My Enemy, My Oppressor, My Love
author: Kara Walker
description: Walker's silhouettes cut through American history with surgical precision. Race, violence, desire, and power rendered in black paper against white walls.

title: The Story of Art
author: E.H. Gombrich
description: From cave paintings to contemporary art — Gombrich's clear, unpretentious account of art history remains a foundational text. A starting point for seeing.

title: The Wretched of the Earth
author: Frantz Fanon
description: Fanon's analysis of decolonisation and the violence of colonial systems. Written in the final year of his life, it remains a central text on liberation and self-determination.

title: Things Fall Apart
author: Chinua Achebe
description: The arrival of colonialism in an Igbo village, told from the inside. Achebe's novel challenged Western narratives of Africa and opened a literary tradition.

title: Citizen: An American Lyric
author: Claudia Rankine
description: Micro-aggressions, systemic racism, and the lived experience of being seen and unseen. Rankine blends poetry, essay, and image into something urgent and new.

title: Silent Spring
author: Rachel Carson
description: The book that launched the environmental movement. Carson documented the devastating effects of pesticides on ecosystems with scientific rigor and literary clarity.

title: The Sixth Extinction
author: Elizabeth Kolbert
description: Kolbert travels to the frontlines of species loss — coral reefs, rainforests, research stations — documenting the mass extinction event we are living through.

title: Pedagogy of the Oppressed
author: Paulo Freire
description: Freire's foundational work on education as liberation. The banking model of education versus dialogue, critical consciousness, and the practice of freedom.

title: The Souls of Black Folk
author: W.E.B. Du Bois
description: Du Bois on double consciousness, the color line, and the inner life of Black Americans at the turn of the century. Essays that defined a century of thought.

title: The Undercommons
author: Stefano Harney & Fred Moten
description: Fugitive planning and Black study. Harney and Moten reimagine the university, debt, and collectivity from below. Dense, generative, uncompromising.

title: Art Since 1900
author: Hal Foster, Rosalind Krauss, Yve-Alain Bois & Benjamin Buchloh
description: A comprehensive survey of modern and contemporary art organised year by year. Four leading art historians map a century of avant-gardes, movements, and ruptures.

title: Interaction of Color
author: Josef Albers
description: Albers' teaching on how colors behave in relation to one another. Not theory but practice — perception, context, and the instability of what we think we see.

title: A Sand County Almanac
author: Aldo Leopold
description: Leopold's meditations on land, wildlife, and the ethical relationship between humans and the natural world. A foundational text of ecological thinking.

title: Capital in the Twenty-First Century
author: Thomas Piketty
description: Piketty's landmark study of wealth inequality across three centuries of data. When the rate of return on capital exceeds economic growth, inequality deepens.

title: Orientalism
author: Edward Said
description: Said's foundational analysis of how the West constructed 'the Orient' as an object of study and domination. A text that reshaped how we think about representation and power.

title: In the Break
author: Fred Moten
description: Moten explores the intersection of Black performance, avant-garde aesthetics, and radical thought. Music, poetry, and visual art as sites of resistance and beauty.

title: Six Years: The Dematerialization of the Art Object
author: Lucy Lippard
description: Lippard's chronicle of conceptual art from 1966 to 1972. Documentation, ideas, and ephemera compiled into a genre-defying account of art escaping its object form.

title: Lose Your Mother
author: Saidiya Hartman
description: Hartman traces the Atlantic slave trade through Ghana, following routes of captivity and erasure. A personal reckoning with history, memory, and the afterlife of slavery.

title: Walden
author: Henry David Thoreau
description: Two years of deliberate living by Walden Pond. Thoreau's experiment in simplicity, observation, and self-reliance remains a provocation against distraction.

title: We Should All Be Feminists
author: Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie
description: A personal, direct essay on gender and equality drawn from Adichie's TEDx talk. Short, clear, and uncompromising.
